questions.question: Explain the FIFO page replacement algorithm with an example.
keyPoints: FIFO replaces the oldest page in memory
Pages are maintained in a queue based on arrival time
Example: with frames 1,2,3 and reference string 1,2,3,4,1,2 - first 1,2,3 cause faults, then 4 replaces 1 (oldest), then 1 replaces 2, etc.

questions.question: What is Belady's Anomaly? Why does FIFO suffer from it while LRU does not?
keyPoints: Belady's Anomaly is increase in page faults when frames are increased
FIFO considers only arrival time, not usage pattern
LRU considers recent usage which correlates with future usage
This is why LRU is stack algorithm and doesn't suffer anomaly

questions.question: Explain the Second Chance (Clock) algorithm and how it improves upon FIFO.
keyPoints: Uses reference bit for each page
If bit is 1: clear it and give second chance
If bit is 0: replace this page
Improves over FIFO by considering recency of use
Less overhead than true LRU

questions.question: Define page fault. Explain the steps OS takes when a page fault occurs.
keyPoints: Page fault occurs when requested page is not in memory
Steps: Check page table, if not valid trigger trap, find free frame or select victim, read page from disk, update page table, restart instruction

questions.question: What is thrashing? Explain its causes and effects on system performance.
keyPoints: Thrashing: excessive page fault activity causing low CPU utilization
Cause: too few frames allocated to processes, high degree of multiprogramming
Effect: processes spend more time in page I/O than computation, system performance degrades severely

questions.question: Differentiate between local and global page replacement algorithms.
keyPoints: Local: replacement limited to pages of same process
Global: any page in memory can be replaced
Local provides better isolation, Global provides better system throughput
Most systems use global replacement for efficiency

questions.question: Explain the Optimal page replacement algorithm and why it cannot be implemented in practice.
keyPoints: Optimal replaces page that will not be used for longest time in future
Gives minimum possible page faults
Cannot be implemented as it requires future knowledge
Used as benchmark to compare other algorithms

questions.question:
Consider the page reference string: 7, 0, 1, 2, 0, 3, 0, 4, 2, 3, 0, 3, 2. Calculate the number of page faults using:
(a) FIFO with 3 frames
(b) LRU with 3 frames
(c) Optimal with 3 frames
Compare the results.
keyPoints: FIFO: 9 page faults (trace through each reference)
LRU: 10 page faults (similar or better than FIFO)
Optimal: 7 page faults (minimum possible)
Optimal gives best results, LRU typically better than FIFO but not always
Shows why Optimal is benchmark and FIFO can be suboptimal

questions.question: Explain the LRU page replacement algorithm. Discuss its advantages and disadvantages. How is it usually implemented in practice?
keyPoints: LRU replaces least recently used page
Advantage: good approximation to Optimal, doesn't suffer Belady's Anomaly
Disadvantage: requires hardware support, overhead of tracking all page references
Implementation: counters for each page OR stack of page references
In practice: approximated using Second Chance/Clock algorithm due to overhead

questions.question: Explain the working set model for controlling thrashing. How does it help in memory management?
keyPoints: Working set: set of pages actively used by a process in time interval τ
Maintain working set in memory to prevent thrashing
If allocated frames < working set size, thrashing occurs
Algorithm monitors working set and allocates frames accordingly
Helps balance between degree of multiprogramming and CPU utilization
